speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with William (Michigan Bill) Stowell, who is known by most people as Michigan Bill, took place at his home in Lewiston on October 29, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager. There is some technical difficulty on this tape.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I come to Canada, but I work all over the Upper Peninsula.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's up with Canada?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I've heard that Upper Peninsula there in Michigan was some logging country. Yeah, it was. It's all cut out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long have you been in Michigan?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It's been three years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you wind up in Michigan? In Michigan?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you wind up there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, just tramping around. I dropped a job, you know. Finally wound up there. After a few years, it's not too long. Maybe six, seven years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: At New York, twenties.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: There's a lot. Let's see. I'll jump out of that country. There were a lot of fins in there. Oh, great. That's what it is. That hook and hand cup, that was all fins. That mining country, that was copper. Yeah, that was 90 percent fins. Yeah, I knew some of them, not all. I didn't remember working the mines, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I was around them a lot. They worked the woods, too, of course. Yeah, there were a lot of fins there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you come out here from there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this country very much like that as far as what the logging life was like?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes. Back to the same when I came here, except the steeper ground, you know. There it was all level. But here's pretty heavy, you know, steep. Different, little different logging, you know. Of course, they used marshes both places then, but they used dogs in this country, you know. Nailed together, you see, and trailed, you know, long trails. Well, 20, 25 at the most, you know, logs. And then it shoots and plumes. They'd shoot them to the plume and then plume them to the river. That's the way they done it here. There's two steeper railroads. There weren't no trucks at that time, so that's the way they did it. You know, a chute. Well, that's the way you went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same thing for like the lockers, same kind of guys out here as it was back there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, there was more farmers back there than here. But otherwise, the same camps, you know, and conditions of difference here, they were better, much better than they were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they better here?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, Lord, yes. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, they had eliminated the top bunks. Well, that was later years they'd done that, but there was double deck bunks there, you know, two in a bunk, and upper and lower. The luggage of everything else was a bad business. That's one reason I got out of there. Not much chance can prove it, but this country here was a way different. You got clean land, you know, every week for your bed. It got to be later years old, bottom bunks, you know, it was no top bunk. Oh, yeah, it's much better here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear much about the IWWs, the work they did to improve their conditions?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes. Yes, they accomplished wonders.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. They did. They were kind of a rough and tough outfit, but they got the work done. They got the job done. Direct action was what it was. There was no political harem scare mixed up in it. It was all direct on the job. That's how it worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about what year was it that you got here in Idaho?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: 27. By that time now, the way I've heard it is it was back in about 1917. Oh, yes, yes, yes. The IWWs had cleaned up the camps. Well, up until 23, the last big strike was in 23, a general strike. For 10 days, that was all. It was just, well, it was for the prisoner to go to jail, you see. Those that were put in jail during these strikes, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean during the earlier ones already? Was to get them out?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, no.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Didn't make it, but they shut everything down for 10 days. But there, that's all it was. They couldn't get it. But they did make a little uprising here in 36, if you remember that. I've heard about that. Yeah, that was for conditions, mostly, and wages. Well, they wanted a minimum of $5 a day. Cleaning up the camps. The camps were getting pretty scrouncy, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And I don't know whether it was won or lost. We were out a couple of months. I was working for the PUT last then, but we were out a couple of months. I don't believe we lost anything. They cleaned the camps up and raised the wages. But they didn't do that until after the strike, you see. They broke the strike, that's what happened. Well, you know, that's the story that I've heard before. That way the company says that it wasn't the IWWs who made the changes because they tried to end the strike and then they make the changes. But it's all one of the same things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, so why are you okay that way?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They didn't do the lack of the strike, but they did it.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. But that was the last feeble attempt that the IWW ever made in this country. I was surprised when I heard about the 36th strike that they were still kicking around all that time because these others, what, the AFL and those groups were getting in and trying to end the CIO too, doing some work. Well, the CIO wasn't too husky yet in this country, you know. They come in afterwards. The AFL got booted out and the CIO took over by a large margin, you know. Well, I don't know. The AFL, who were the guests, they were, well, the CIO was political too, but there there was more so, you know. I never belonged, I belonged to the CIO, but I never did belong to the AFL in the woods, you know. I belonged to the pilot driver, like that. Well, I had the idea that the AFL was more like a craft union than it was a real industrial union. Sure, sure it was. It was a craft union, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did that way make more sense to me, the CIO would be the organized and lumbered industry?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. It was less of a removal of the two, but I don't know. Being that I had belonged to the IWW, I couldn't see much detail about it. No, I couldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think made the IWW so much a better union than the CIO?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, you could act more or less without all these executive boards and the like of that, you know. You could, all the meetings can't be decided to do something and majority rules take a vote and if the majority wanted it, why that's the way it was. It went as far as the crew was concerned. We didn't always win, but it was an effort anyway.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That was what I like about it, these other organizations are too much political, they have high salaries, big shots. All that is always. It don't work with a common stiff, you know. I don't figure it does.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you ever in camps where you guys took a vote and then went out on strike?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yes. Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of reason would make you decide you had to do that?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, well, the different reasons. Mostly, especially the IWW, they were mostly for conditions, you know. Working conditions and living conditions. Get away from them old double bunks and lousy camps and bum chuck and all that, you know. Trying to get something better to, well, better living is what it was really. They never did strike too much for money because they take that away from you anyway, you know. That money.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I don't think the course of the IWW has never come back, you know. Not as the IWW. They're done.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They were revolutionary, you know. That was the core of the Constitution.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And revolutionary organization, at this day and age, won't get along very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it made more sense in the old days than it would make nowadays?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, that's kind of a hard question to answer. I wouldn't attempt to answer it.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Conditions have changed a lot. I don't know. I sometimes when I look at history, I sometimes wonder, you know, whether it's changed all that much or whether it's... People have changed, you know. People have changed an awful lot. Oh, yes. That's what makes it, I think. It's the people themselves. You see, the lumberjack was always a single man, a footloose man, you know. He didn't give a goddamn for anything, you know. He had nothing to lose and the world to gain. So that's what made him that way, I believe. I'm pretty sure that's the way it was for me. It'd be a camp for, say, 150 men in a camp. They might be 2 % married or maybe 1 % all single.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And they had no responsibility except themselves. And it would make a gain for them.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Now you see they're all married. They got responsibilities. They had payments on cars and homes and TVs and whatnot. Them days, the older days, all of them had his pecs, I guess, what was on his back, that's all.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It seems so different than it is when I think about it in the way you're saying, then and now. I can't even think of how the situation would arise, that so many men would be able to be single and not be tied down by anything.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It's hard to figure that out, isn't it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I don't know why they never got married then. I can't figure that out. Well, I never did, but I would just go, well, from job to job, you know, and I didn't have no responsibility. I couldn't afford to have any unless I wanted to settle down and take less money for some other job. We always made a little more than the town workers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that? Because you guys could go anyplace where there was a job?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Wherever the money was, yeah. Well, in the gyppo system, I mean, you know, of course, that thing, everything gave a minimum wage, but it was awful small. Everybody wanted a gyppo. And they always made a story about where you'd come out the third about the regular wages anyway, you see, by going the limit, you know, doing the upmost. You're very bad. Every day you had to, you had to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a, is that a good deal?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, it sounds like you... Oh, yes, sure. No, that wasn't a good deal for us at all. No, we tried to abolish it, but it... you couldn't. I don't know. There's too many that wanted it that way. You know, really wanted it that way. And they're right down their heart. They wanted that. I mean, good to military union men, too, they weren't really what I'd call good military men, but that if there was any trouble at all, they were always ready to help out. They, most of them wanted a gyppo system. A little more money. You know, that's what drives people out of that money. Yeah. Yeah. Sure. Well, for a few years, or a guy can break his back working to be young, and, you know, and really can put out the work, but it seems like it's going to catch up with him after a while. Well, he does, you know, and that's what he does. Sure. You may think you can do it, but you can't. Now he'll poop out pretty goddamn sudden like when you get older. That's awful hard work. In the old days, you know, everything was handwork. Falling was all handwork, bucking was all handwork. And the skidding was all well, the horses, you know, but P .B. Work, you know, you had all that to do. That's what it did, work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the guys at the WWF position on that? Did they say the gyppo was no good?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. I have to stand. They took absolutely no good, no. It seemed to me that a guy who, if you really believed in a, you know, in a union, then the gyppo would be very unequal, because the guys that could put out the work would make a lot better than, you know, the gyppo. Old -timers would say you couldn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes, but when you got old enough, you quit your phone, though. You couldn't. That was the way that worked. But, well, you were good up to 50 years old, you know. You knew a lot of tricks, a lot of shortcuts by that time. You know, you didn't know when you were younger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When? When?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Just generally speaking, you know, I can't believe. The way to do the work and, you mean, like, you learned, you really mastered the whole job. Yeah, sure. That's it. That's it. Yeah, that's my idea, yes. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It, uh, well...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Just as I say, you learned a lot of tricks and shortcuts, and it all helped out at the end of the day, you know. You accomplished a little more than the absolute green one, and done it just about as easy, public.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I have heard that most of the early lumberjacks around this country were old. I have heard that most of the early lumberjacks out in this country, they were old men, or older men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true, so far as you know, in the early days?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: There were a lot of old men, yes. There were a lot of them. When they come to this country, you know, from the east, they were all from the east, practically. Eastern Canada, and the Midwestern states, from Wisconsin, Minnesota, Michigan. That's where most of them were from when I came out here. They were all men all along in their years, seeing. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were one of the young ones when they came out?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, sure.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That was only about twenty -three, I think, when I went.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But all I ever did was work in the woods, you know, and I was a kid at home. That's all it was. Well, the old man had temper, you know. Old sawmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Old water power sawmill.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: He must have seen it ever since you were old enough to see things. Yeah, sure. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he let you work on it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, no. Oh, no. No, I just was around it, you know. I never, I never done nothing around it. I'd pile a little lumber or something like that. Worked a pound a little bit, too. I was going to school then. I didn't do much work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that in Canada?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Kind of where I was in Quebec. The province of Quebec. A little small town about Sarge Piers, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of inter -operations did he have there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Or would he employ a... Oh, no. He had a sawyer, that's all. No. The rest of it was. That was a neighbor. 39. He was working for lumber.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: He had... Fix.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do to get some lumber?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you come down to the States because there was more work?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No. No, I come down to go to school. Huh. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You were still just a... No, just a kid. Yeah. I was... Seven, I guess.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: When you guys were in camp, did you have...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much reading material around?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No. Not years ago there was none. Except what somebody brought in there. You take like, Bradbury now, Howard Bradbury. He was awful good. He was superintendent up here, you know. He'd always come in with a little magazine and stuff like that. But otherwise there was nothing. Nobody brought anything with him. And they'd come in. Daily papers, that was out, you know. There was no such a thing. The mail would come in. Want the week maybe or something like that. Pack horse, you know. Pack string and bring it in. And, well, if you had any mail, you'd get it. If you didn't, of course you didn't. I never had no mail coming around like that. But I used to like it all over. The paper wasn't a while. Everybody did. But like sports or something like that. I used to be lost. I like baseball, you know. And I used to follow that pretty close. But I'd be... Everything would go right by me. You'd get out and be a damn World Series already. The Overlighter. I used to take time off for the series sometimes and come out.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But... You could do that pretty easy. I mean, you could leave if you wanted to and come back. Oh, Christ, you'd leave anytime. That's a pretty good deal. Well, some of them guys didn't stay over ten days anyway. You know. It's enough to make a road to take and move on. But I never was that way. If I got a job that I liked making with money, I'd stay with it. So, you know, quite a while. But then when I went to town after I went broke, I'd always go someplace else. You know. Move around a little bit. See how they done things other places.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, when do you figure on completing this book?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, it's going to be probably a couple of years before. Oh. Quite a while. Well, there's more than you working on it. No, just me. Just you. Yeah. That's why it takes so long.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But it's going from camp to camp now. That's something that you really like to do, but most of the guys like to move around like that. Oh, probably half of them, yeah. Some of them used to stay, but probably fifty percent of them. They would, what they called jumping jacks, you know. They'd work a few days and go. Yeah, there were lots of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you do it all, was it always by yourself or did you chum around with a, would you go with another guy?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, when there's dip on come out, you know, of course that was here when I come to this country. But you always had a partner, you know. If you were following while you had a partner, you see. You usually travel with them or pick somebody else up. Or if you went into the camp alone, the poo should make you up with somebody, you know, if he wanted you to go to work. I traveled around with the guy, oh, maybe two or three years at a time. Same guy, you know, I worked with him. Some guy's life is a good man, you know, and I would stay with him. And we were someone who'd get along with you. That way, it made work easier and you know what he was going to do all the time. That way, why you'd keep your partner, you see. I waited in town for several days for a guy to get sober enough to go out, you know. Or he'd wait for me or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was it, was, now, there are other jobs besides falling, you needed two men, huh?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah, skiddin'.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two men?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, Teamster and swamper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do a lot of both of them?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Both fallin' and skiddin'?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, no, no, I didn't do any fallin'.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you did the skiddin' and swamperin' and things?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: A lot of that's about all I did. That's for years, you know, it was a pretty fair money and I don't know, I like horses. So I stayed with it. Well, they did that by markin' out strips.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They did that by markin' out strips and you took a strip. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you'd done that for so much on the, so much for the whole strip or did they do it by the thousand?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, it was by the thousand which used, the way it used to be, a strip would be all blazed out, you know. You know, up this draw or someplace. And it would run back to the ridge and, depends on what the timber was and everything, it would all be felled, you see, ahead, you know. And they'd set a price on it. You'd go out and look at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like you'd come into camp this afternoon, supposed to come around and say, you want to go work?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Well, I'll come out and I'll show you a strip. So he'd take you out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wouldn't go over the strip, would he?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But he'd take you down the flume or up the chute or wherever the hell it was and say, there you are. It's a line here and another line's up there. You'll see it looking over. Well, then you would, you see.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And he'd already chose a price, you see.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Then you'd go back to camp and probably the next morning he'd say, well, you're going to take that patch?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, you'd say, you got to raise the ante a little bit or something like that, you see. Well, I can't go no higher. Well, then you'd take it or leave it, you see. You'd probably leave it to him, go. But if he'd raise it two bits or something, say the price was two and a half thousand or three or four or five or something, if you wanted four bits or a dollar or two bits more, why you'd tell him. If he'd seen fit to give it to you, he wouldn't. If he didn't, he wouldn't. You'd go or take it, one or the other, whatever he wanted to do. You could size it up pretty good without doing it, on how much it'd take you to make good money on it. You could just tell from looking at it. If you had a partner that you knew and knew what he could do, you could tell just about how close you'd come. After you, you know, I'd been at it quite a while and I knew just about how I'd come out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what was the difference? Did it vary a lot, how much money you'd be able to make on gyppoing? I mean, did it change a lot or was it pretty much set no matter?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, no. Like this part, it was pretty much, they'd like to make so much. If you went over that, they'd cut the price. After you started on the strip, some outfits didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd cut it while you were still on the strip?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It sounds like it was... You signed a contract, but it didn't mean nothing, you know. Except if you killed a horse or something like that, you had to pay for him, you know. A team of horses would buy you $500. That would standard value of them. But if you killed one or both of them, that didn't happen very, very, very soon. But if you broke a leg or something like a mane horse's leg or something like that, they'd naturally destroy him, you see. But that didn't happen, you know, with Christ.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like if they'd change a price in the middle of doing a strip, then it didn't pay for you to work too hard?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well... You worked too hard, you made too much money. It'd take like at Bovill, at one time you could make about $100 a month, $105, $110 or $90, something like that. That would just stay in between there. And there was nobody who went over that, regardless of the price.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But from this, Clearwater started up, they were a little different. Nobody made the same. You could... You got a good strip, a big enough strip that you could lay it out properly and go ahead, you see, what you figured. Do your best advantage, you know. Leave some sort all in, take the long with the short, and keep it mixed up. You could come out with a Puddy Fair average. That's the way I always did it, but most of them did it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this idea to mix short and longs?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Why... Well, there was some longs getting, you know, maybe... Well, they've skidded 3 ,000 feet, 3 ,500 feet, you know. And some of them would be a couple hundred feet. You'd mix them. Yeah. That's right. Like, if you didn't have time to make a trip, you see, like, maybe you say you was skittin' 4 trips a day, maybe using dogs, you see. You know what them dogs are, you know. Well, you'd maybe say it was steep ground, you was... Maybe using 25 dogs. Well, if you was making 4 trips and you couldn't get... You'd get caught short some day and you couldn't make 4. Well, you'd jump in on the short haul, leave the swamper up in the woods and let him stay there. You'd pick up some handy stuff and see enough to keep your average up. That's the way that worked. Did they pay you bonus on the... Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you hit a certain amount every day, you know they've done it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: The Winton Lumber Company, they were out of the Coeur d 'Alene. They set a price, it didn't change. They might raise it. If you weren't making it and they wanted you to stay, they might raise it. But otherwise it didn't change and if you made $100 a day, they paid you. But it wasn't very... Nobody ever did that, you know. But I mean, regardless of what you were making, they'd leave the price after they set it. Well, that sounds really bad to me. Potlatch changing the price in the middle. I mean, they make a deal, it ought to be a deal. Yeah, I know. I didn't say they cut the price, but they raised it, you see, if you weren't making it on the strip. I never knew to cut it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking about potlatch?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Oh, they didn't raise it. If you weren't making it. Well, that's different. That's okay, then. Yeah. See, I've heard it said that if you made good on one, the next one they'd try to cut you back so you wouldn't... Well, if you took another one. Right. Yeah. That's what I've heard. Oh, they all tried that. Yeah, that was customary. That'd be a good reason for leaving to go into another place. Yeah, well, that's usually what they did. But most guys quit after they put a strip in anyhow, with any size way. Say a couple of months, you know. That much. That long. Yeah, when they got the strip in, they'd go. And when they got ready to go, they'd go back maybe someplace else or come back there. Didn't make any difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how big a strip are we talking?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It sounds like quite a bit of... Oh, some strip, a hundred thousand and somewhere a million. How many acres would that... Oh, I wouldn't know. I wouldn't know about the acreage. That sounds pretty big. A couple of months is a lot of work. Yeah, but I know guys when the camp took a million feet. All summer. All summer long. Maybe they wouldn't get it then. But they knew who they were giving it to, you see. They were pretty steady guys, you see. They wouldn't give it to someone that was going to just dig in a little while there and then we'd give it to some of them. How big would...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men would be in these camps, guys working on the strips?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In a camp?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, that's about there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these camps tent or car camps or what?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Both. Frame, tent, and cars. Regular camps, sometimes it was car camps. But like the flume camps, they were all either ride camps, you know, tents or frames. Part of that had a couple of log camps, built a couple of log camps. They were just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they older camps that were standing there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Them early camps were. Them real early camps were log camps. Oh, yeah, sure, they all were. But no, they built Camp G up here on... Yeah. Silver Creek. And that was a log camp. Yeah, that was a built in 1931. Huh. Yeah. Well, what did you think of the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any one kind of them camps that was usually better or better to live in?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And others?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Which was the... Well, the way it was made was whenever there was a good cook, that's where I'd go. I used to kind of keep track of the cooks. There were a lot of bum ones and a lot of good ones then, you know. If I lived in good ones, they were well -known, so were the bum ones. I used to kind of keep track of the good ones and go where they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Shorty Justice?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yes. I talked to him in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he living?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Sure is. Jesus Christ. Yeah, he cooked last time I worked. He was cooking at Camp 5 out of Bovill on the highway. It was right on the highway. Red Cliff Runach. Five, Red Cliff. He was supposed to have been one of pretty good cooks. Oh, yes, he was a good cook. Yeah, sure he was. Hell, yes. I love his chuck. He's living. He must be pretty old. Oh, he's about 80. Yeah. You know, he was... You talked to him in Spokane. After he left his country, he went and he was purchasing agent for the RID Path there in Spokane for that big hotel. Yeah. That's what he'd done. He bought the chuck. Yeah, he bought it for him. But that was just a sideline with him. He wasn't doing that much nothing full -time. I don't know what else he was doing. No, he wasn't doing nothing else. That's after he retired. Yeah, from down here. Yeah. Well, there was a really big difference between the good chuck and the bad. Oh, yeah. A world of difference. Yeah, a bum cook, you know, he wasted so much, you know. Garbage cans full all the time. For a good cook, he'd take them leftovers and he'd do something with them. And they'd be good, you know. Fred Thomas, he used to live in Coeur d 'Alene. He must be dead. He was an awful good cook. He used to cook on Marble Creek on the drive. You know, Marble Creek is... Well, they'd go over there for quite a few years. The Bruckleed Stember Company. And I see him poached eggs right on the bank of the river for 150 men in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's something, isn't it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: By Jesus. Oh, he was a dandy cook and a good guy. I haven't seen him since he left over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of outfit did they have to cook on on the River Drive?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They didn't have a full camp cook outfit. They had to move the damn thing. Well, yeah. Well, you'd either throw everything in a couple of boats... Or put it on a pack string.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had big heavy cook stoves?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Sure. Like this river here, you know. Everything... The cook had it good here. A lot of them didn't like it. They had everything right in front of them all the time. You know, it was only 30 men, you know. But... Well, they could bake bread while you were moving.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You'd take, like, Marble Creek and the Coeur d 'Alene River and them... Priest River and them, they would... Well, they'd throw everything in a boat and move maybe five miles, six miles, three miles or something. It depends on the river. Down river. And then a cook would have to come and have supper ready for 100 men, maybe from 3 o 'clock on in the afternoon. They'd come and set that camp up. That was something. Of course, the push. He'd always give the cook lots of help, you know. Off of the dry... Off of the vehicle, you know. And the guys liked to do that, too. You know, it was a change, you know. You'd take, like... Well, after you've been at that old drive... I drove 100 days one time. 100 days without... Well, just that every day, the same goddamn thing. Christ's feet sore. And we had the camp on the bank. We'd move five, six miles, back string. Well, we got to take that stove off, you know. Oh, yeah. Well, you'd have to put that on mule. Pretty hard to get it balanced up, you know. You'd overload the old mule, you know, what a big stove would wait. Yeah. Well, they used to have packed saddles. They were built like a sawhorse, you know. Take everything off of the stove, you could then put it right on top of it, and lash it down, you know. But they were big, strong mules, good mules. Just like the government had, you know, the Forest Service, they got good mules, too.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But, uh... Jesus Christ, that was an awful job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, the moving that camp?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Not every day. Not every day, no. Oh, that's right. You'd move it whenever you needed to. Whenever you got too far away, you would hike.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It's all hike and chance, ain't it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: In fact, lately here, the last few years I was down here, they had jet boats on this river. Christ, you didn't hike. No place. Jumping along those up rivers, down river, anyways. Right. Nothing to it. Them young fellas, kicking. It must have been a whole day proposition when you had to break the camp down and move it, set it up and do it. Oh, yeah. You'd take a lunch that day, you see. The cookers have a lunch ready for you in the morning, the paper sack. You'd take that lunch that day, that's the only day you took a lunch, the other way you come in. But that day you took a lunch. But that didn't seem very good. This time went on, you know, and there were two or three moves, four or five moves, and pretty soon there was only two or three more moves to make. That's the way I looked at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be maybe once a week or twice a week? You'd be one of moving that thing?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Depends on the water. How fast you were moving on that river.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, like on the drive, when you worked 100 days, would you be doing much different work from day to day, or would it wind up being the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have one job? Would you be set on the same job the whole trip? Like what would the job be? You'd be a pee -pee man?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I never run both. Oh, I just dinged around a little bit, but I never... I was always a pee -pee. I don't know why I... I never carried around a boat. Well, there's too much responsibility. They'd swamp them boats, too, you know. Lose men. Jesus Christ. Now I never want nothing to do with that.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, working on the PV, did you have much...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wouldn't it have been pretty tricky when you were breaking a jam?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, I had no business. If you wanted a drift, you had to be able to take care of yourself. You know, what I mean. Like if you got... If a chunk broke off or something, and the boat couldn't get to you, it was up to you. The crew might scatter, you see. You might be a man here and a man over here. But you can't get them all. If somebody got the rider out, you know, they'd usually pick up the ones they figured were the least capable, you know, and the hell with the other son of them. They'd let him go, we'd make her. That's the way they figured it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do if you got cut a drift like that? You'd be on a log and you'd just have to hang onto it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, it stayed with the corset. Didn't seem to bother them guys much, you know. I was the same way. If you went a drift, you went a drift. That was up to you then. A lot of them did the purpose, to get out of work. Sure, to ride around with a bender to jump off. If it's a nice sunny day, lay down. Sure, I've done it myself. Not a thousand times, but I've did it quite often. If I didn't feel like working. But I know, I kind of like to work. I stick around and be as people as opposed to going, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, I think about living in a camp with 150 men for months at a time. And I think about that. I never had to live that close to men all the time, and get along and all that. I used to, short periods of time, you know. It's a little like the Army that way, to all be in a place together all the time. I was thinking about that.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You'd have to be able to all get along pretty good, I think. Or it'd be pretty easy to get a lot of tension going. Well, nowadays I believe there would be. But then, at that time, it wasn't. But nowadays I believe people are different. I don't believe these young fellas could do that. I don't know why it makes me think that, but I don't believe they could. I think there'd be more 50 cops going on like that, you know. Maybe they're more selfish, I don't know. It seems like you're not as used to thinking about considering other people, maybe. Maybe that's part of it. I don't know. I don't know. I couldn't say. But I kind of think I've often thought about that. I believe you put a bunch of men together for six months at a time. Of course they work every day. They get rid of their energy that way, you know. But otherwise I don't know how it would work out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it'd be more or less about being in prison, wouldn't it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, prison is... Prison, you can't go ride a log around the whole thing. Oh, no, no, no. But I mean, it'd be the same thing every day, every day, every day, you know. Of course you've been, like, come home with night, you've been relaxed, you've washed clothes, played poker. You're tired anyway, you know, from working all day. First thing you know, it's bedtime, and down you go again. But I don't know, these young fellows nowadays, I can't seem doing that. I think they'd be... They kind of... It seems like... Well, they don't work as hard when they do work. They seem like they always want to go someplace, you know. Go, go, go. And like, they've all got cars now, you know. Yeah, now that's got to be part of it. To be back there and isolated for a long time, that drive... That drive a guy's crazy. Yeah. I don't mind. I've been, you know, I've worked on lookouts and that kind of thing, and been up there for months, and I kind of enjoy it. But then you're kind of by yourself, too. Oh, I don't like being alone.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, it's been a while since I have been. It's hard to know. Back for a while I didn't mind it, but I don't know how I feel about it now. Yeah, it's kind of hard to say. But you sure weren't alone in the woods with all that. Oh, Christ Jesus, you know, there's always something going on. You know, you could always... And at night there'd always be some new guys coming, you know. Guys that you knew, and you'd get to talking to them and find out how it's going on here and there around the country, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You sure?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That was quite a... I used to always look for who'd come around in the evening, you know, new guys. BS with them. If they didn't go to work, you'd always slip them a little money, you know. 10 or 20 or something, you know, to go on, you see. You know, they'd pay you back maybe five years from then. And whenever they met you and had it, that's when you'd get it. But they wouldn't forget. Oh no, oh no, oh no. Christ, no. No, if you ever met them and they had her, you'd get her. And if they didn't, average you wouldn't. That's all there was to it. Simple as that. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: What about these... the kind of guys that... I'm sure there must have been some guys in the camp that really were kind of jerks. You know, guys that just... There must have been a few guys that really were just no good. Yeah, sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about them guys? Did they get kicked out of camp?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah. Them old time pushes would get away, so one of them might quit, you know. There were some of them, they would try them at different jobs, you know. Trying to find out something they could do. And eventually they'd... either let them go over and find something they could do. Them old clamp pushes were pretty fair. A lot of them were tough, you know, to... out, you know. But a lot of them were good. They're reasonable men, you know. They... I know a guy, I'm like a quick push. You could go there and lean up against an old stump all summer and he wouldn't fire you. But he wouldn't hire you back the next few years. He would stay there as long as he wanted to. But, of course, he had to do a little something, but... No matter how much it was, if you were working by the day, he'd leave you there. He would never tell you to go. Or we might make it a little miserable sometimes for you, but you never can. But the next spring, of course, or whenever he would... No, he used to come around him, you know, he wouldn't hire you. Sounds like a pretty nice guy. Oh, yeah, sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Fife, Steve Fife. Yeah, that was Micah Crete. Well, what about a guy that kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did some jacks get bad reputation with other men? I mean, did you get an idea that this guy was no good and he'd stay away from him?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. He was pretty much a loner than... I'd imagine. They would never have had to do with him. They didn't want to work with him. If they did, they'd do something just drive him off the job, you know, and try to kill him, maybe. You can't tell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would it take to get a reputation like that? To steal or lie or what?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Just no good. Don't pay your bills. Not pay any debts. Never give anybody any help that needed it, you know. You were in a position to help him. Something like that that would put the engine sign on him. Mm -hmm. You know, I've been thinking about the Wobblies since we talked before. It's just really interesting to me that union. I mean, it just sounds like a good union to me. I like the sound of the way they were democratic and the way they really seemed to be out for the men and stay out themselves. They were real good. The only reason the IWW isn't in force today, it was revolutionary. You see, they believed in the overthrow of the government by force. That was right in the preamble, the Constitution.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember what that first one was like they had on the river?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes, sure, I do. I remember all about it.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Who the hell had that whip?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Joshua Webb. I think he must be dead, though, we have this. But they drove from Big Island then. You see, the river that Big Island, them being seen, them can't put that timber in. All flumed. And then the next spring,

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: the next spring they drove from Beaver Creek. They had gotten into the river and started camps up there. And old Brooks brought that drive down as far as Big Island and Webb took it from there on in. But then the next year, old Brooks went all the way through. They had one again, as you see. Webb had one again, too. But Brooks had to build them two up on the north, way up the north part. But his were just kind of a skimpy affair compared to what old Webb had. Webb had regular, I guess you could cross the hills now. They were all square -tempered, boning together. But the ones, old Brooks built, there were no bolts or spikes or nothing in them, just all made out of wood, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They were different lengths of cedar, kind of interlocked this way, you know. And they're real flexible. Stuff about, you know, 18, 20 inches on the butt, it was. 40 and 60 feet long, they were. And they were held together by, they were about 85 feet long, possibly. About 22 feet wide. I helped build them. They were held together by what they called wits. They was wild cherries. And they laid these miners across.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These what?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: These miners, you know. They were mostly mined maple. And these whole living boards, you know, these embers, because this deer, that the raft was big out, was bored, you see. And then they drove a, they put this witting and a square peg with it, you know. And around 0 .5 -8 of the size. And that's what held it together. Just this wittle with this lined maple with binders. And you couldn't, you had to, if you wanted to dismantle them, you had to cut them up. You couldn't, there was nothing coming apart otherwise. So it was pretty dumb. Oh, yeah. But flexible, you know. In rough water, they oscillate, you know, up and down. So there was no bridge in them that way. This was the first drive. Yeah, they used them all the time after that. Same kind. Same kind, right after the last drive. Well, were the, except they put rubber on. They had rubber, you see, the last, they had to, oh, I don't know, 10 years maybe, they had the, the contoons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think, huh? This, the first drive, were they well, well -constructed, well -made?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they service them all the time?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Yes, yes, yes, truly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the first drive go?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, I take it they hadn't had, they never drove them down to Clearwater before, so they must have been a new experience for them. Well, there was an old guy by the name of Piper. He did years ago. He run the first rafts, and he knew the river. He lived on the river. He was an old prospector, but he wrapped it, the cedar poles on that river. When they used to, well, I call it pirating or hydrating or whatever the hell you want to call it, before this dam was built, you know, they'd make up wood rafts and cedar rafts and run them down into the snake and on down and sell them, you see. Well, he used to do that with his business. He'd done that. Well, he knew every pebble in that Northport, and he run the first rafts down for the company. He helped build them. He built them, you know. He was a super writer. He held a building, and he chose a power builder man to run. So that's how they made it with that project. Today I remind you of his job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he working for that before, or was he independent of gyppo?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, he was independent of gyppo.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: He used to make up two or four cedar rafts a year, you know. And that's before the company started writing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this pirating, was this really company timber and man was taking it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, anybody. On the bank of the river, you know, standing close to the bank. Anybody. Yeah, they did a lot of it, yeah. Everybody was doing this. Worshann, cedar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make a living that way?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, cedar you could, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to come down on your raft, make sure it made it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, oh yeah. I wonder where they sold, where they sold it, how far down they had to go. Well, I don't know. A lot of them were sold and made up in a post, cedar post, you know. They had a premium down the river, you know. There's no timber down there, you know, on that snake, you know what that is. Oh yeah. You probably wouldn't have to go too far once you get back in it. Yeah, tumbleblades is all it is. But yeah, they made a living out of it. This old piper was a fine old guy. They found him dead in his cabin. That way he'd go over here, that spring, he was a funny old guy. He'd never come to town. He'd get out here, he'd land out here at the mill with them rafts, and that is when he was driving by the company, you know, and hit tight back upriver. And he was a ragged old thing, you know. I owned a gear, went to a barber and like that. He was just an old hermit, but he was. But a good old guy, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever talk to him and know how long he'd been in this country?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes, he'd been here for years. Yeah, he built, I don't know how many cabins he built on that north part. He'd trap the top of the two of you, you know. And he'd get, well, he'd wander off, you know, go and look, and he'd just trap a light over it, and he'd have a cabin to stay in. He built, you know, just lean tools and cabins of all kinds up and down that north part. There was another guy come on after him, a side of Joe Cuckney, his name was. He built a half a dozen cabins too, but he built a pedal. He made a living track, and he'd work on the driver in the spring. He was a good driver, very French Canadian, real good. And he died up there, right in the gutter, there in the back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the side of Joe come from? He didn't talk?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No one talked. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At all?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, he'd say, well, you're true, of course, you know, but he wouldn't try to keep the conversation or not and go, and he'd just sit there. I used to bump and see him, you know, after he had quit working, he was too old to work. And we'd be waiting, I used to go in a little earlier than the driving crew, you know, there'd be five or six of us who wouldn't build him raps, you know. And, oh, he lived about a mile from where we'd be building, you know. I'd bump and see him. He'd sit there, he wouldn't talk. I'd keep on talking, you know, and he'd ask to know what's all he'd say. There was nothing in the track, you know, an old fine band, a couple old kettles of some kind. I wouldn't bump. I said, well, I'm going to go, Joe. Oh, sit down, sit down. So I'd have to sit another half hour. He would never sing up. I'd finally get out of there. But he died right there. He never come out. Same thing as his piper. He'd be trapped and he'd done the river drives, too. Just the piper.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Yeah. He didn't drive too long. He was down there about five years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Piper, you mean?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. He was back up there one spring and they found him dead in the doorway of his shack. They don't know whether he's fair -bearded or foul or what happened. I don't think it could be foul because there's nobody ever around there. I think he just died. He pretty well decomposed when they found him. This piper. Yeah. Go ahead. Go ahead, Piper. Let's see who took them raps then, Frank Larkins. He dead, too, though. He was on them then. He was good as old does. And then they had different ones after that.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I used to work on them all the time, help move them, you know. But I never did run them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When that first year, how many of them did they have?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Raps. Yeah. They had one, two, three. They had a sleeping raft, a cook raft, and a chuck raft. You see, they couldn't get no chuck in. There was no roads in there then until they got to Big Island. See, there was, I don't know, 60 miles there, I guess. And the rear was heavy, and you were moving slow, you know. That is, say, a mile a day or something like that, you know. They took a lot of chuck. That's where they had the chuck raft. They had a regular crew that, well, there was one man that'd run the raft, then they'd pick up, he'd pick up bin out of all the crew, you see, to help it move. And they usually got a day off that. Well, it took a day. There was a lot of height, and you see, you stay, you move five miles, you had to hike 15 miles, you see, back three different times.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And then if you missed the landing or something, which we did, that caused more complications then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you do it to this landing? You had it roped back or something?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, no, oh, no, you're impossible to do that. I was without a question. No, you went to the next one, or you got in some place where you thought you could. This old piper knew where we bend in the river, you know. And so this is Frank Larkins. They were lucky that way. They always had someone that knew.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But later on when they got motors on them, they put motors on them rubber rafts. That was the first one they put the motors on. You see, it's nothing to do with them. Except for the groundwater and them. They didn't draw that much water from the pontoons. They could go up river and place it with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could, how many would get stopped anyways? That is not any place, but if the water were to, what is it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That's a might to a halt. Yeah, that was, that simplified everything as long as you're...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, did, how many men would run that raft in the old days? Would it just be one? How many men would move it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, they'd be usually five.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had these poles?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, sweeps. Long oars, you know. There was an 18 -foot blade on about a 40 -foot stem. The bowboard usually had three men. If you run back and forth across with it, you see, if you would... The guy had run from the stern, you know, the pilot. He'd call the shots from the stern. If he wanted to go over to the right, why, he'd holler, right. And then you'd, you'd always in position anyhow, you see. Then you'd dip your oar and give her a gut to the right, you see. The oar would be about all this high. Above your head there. That is the back end of it, you know, would be the shorter man would be... Down the ground. Goes through the edge. Yeah, he'd be reaching up. But you could do... You could bring up the time. You'd take quite a few clubs sometime, you know. Landing was about the worst part of it. They'd run through an ordinary eddy, you know. They were so heavy. If you went in the upper end of the eddy, they'd either swing on you or run through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If it wasn't a real strong eddy, why'd they swing on you?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, they'd load up the water in front, you know, the water'd be up to here. And then you'd swing, and there you were, you would be held straight through.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It used to be trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you do?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, try to straighten it out. You couldn't run from the stern because the stern was loaded. The way down the stern was in the water, you know. They would bother over the stern, you see. It was too heavy to handle. The bow was up. You had to work it in the bow. You'd turn again if you could to get the bow ahead. And then, by the land. If you got them... Well, you had two rafts, say, a sleeping raft and a cook raft and a chuck raft. Well, if you got the... You had to have them together, you know. At night, when the crew come in, you... If you didn't have them, they couldn't be a mile apart, you know. You missed the one, you'd have to pass it up with the other one and go where the first one was. And then maybe you'd have to hike three or four miles back to the rear in the morning, you see. That was... That was no good. They used to try to keep them as close to the rear as possible, you know. That was their idea. Eliminate that hiking, you know. Because when you're marching, you ain't fighting, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what kind of a spot did the pilot have to pick out on the river? Would it have to be a real sizable cove or something like that?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes, that would be the best. And the bank, you know, tried to get a place. He wouldn't run into a bus pile or nothing. You know, you had to wash sinks and stuff to set out on the bank and build fires with the crew at night. And he had places, you know. After the first couple of years, they had places, regular places, where they landed at the roof. Something... If the rear was moving past, they'd miss one or two of them, you see. Otherwise, they'd run in there and tie up, you know. Well, we used to have snub lines, and we had an old boat, an old battle. We'd go ahead and set this snub line, you see. It was just a long... There were two -inch lines, I think, with a bow in it, you know. And you'd set that and put it in a stick or something, so you could see it, you know. So the guy had to bail off of that raft, because it would be too, too long, you see. And you had a shorter line on the raft, maybe 30 feet, with a big snap in it, you see. A big iron snap that long with a spring in it, you know. Well, you'd grab that and bail off, you know, the guy that was supposed to do the snub. And there was always one good act of man doing that, you know. And you'd grab this line with a bow in it, and there was a raft on a tree, you see. One raft is a rule, a tree or a stump or something. Well, you'd make the joint, then, you see, with the big snap in the bow. And then you'd drop that and grab the other line, you see, and pay out a little slack. And if you snub it up real tight, and it was in straight water, you know, it would take the stern of the raft down and probably break the line at that end, too. But they could just give a little slack, you know, on the stump, and you could snub at any time you wanted to as far as if you had to hitch or do it, but, you know. But you... That spring cushioned... No, no, that spring, that spring would just keep the rock. And then you'd bring the lines together. Oh, okay. It wasn't a coil spring. It was just a spring that kept the lines. After it got in the snap, you know, it couldn't fly out. No, there was no cord. Oh, it sounds like you didn't have... you had no time to play with it like that. Oh, no, no, no, no. It had to be done. If you missed, you was looking at them going around the bend, and there you were. You didn't know where the hell they were going. And you made a kind of a leap out of yourself, too. That was part of it. They'd kid you about that, you know. And maybe find someone else to do it, if you missed too often.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Now, the deal on this was that they would take one raft at a time, down the... and then go back to the next one. But when they got the rubber, you know, that's for last just a few years they had that rubber. But that was all in one. That was 118 feet long. Motor on each end. Hook house in the middle. Sleeping wire... as sleepy as I'll each end, you see. It was in three sections. And they was awful flexible, little things. Rough to ride, you know. Rough water, just like a bunker would, jumping around. But when you landed at that thing, everything was there. You didn't have to go back after nothing. I'll bet the old timers must have really thought that was quite a change. Oh, yeah. You fell in on the drive, you never could imagine what them old days were like. No, geez. I remember one time we missed... we missed with the Stigner Wanigan at Roberson Island. That's just above the Little North Fork. And we never got tied up when we got to Big Island. I don't know what the hell the idea was. It was 30 miles there.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I was new on that too. I didn't know where the hell it was going. But the pilot did, Lark and T. No, but I don't know. Nobody ever did find out. He got canned anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For that?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: For that. Well, he did about... When we got to Big Island, there was another raspman there. But I hiked back up. I don't know. We had to sleep on the bank then.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: We had 12 days on that bar and we slept on the bank. We had the cook raft, you see. We landed at the cook raft. Another crew moved there. They landed. No trouble, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It took 12 days to get to the sleeping raft?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Sleep on the bank every night. And no blankets much. They built a big fire along the way to that house. You know, they would have two or three men cutting wood all day. Keep your feet warm. There was snow on the ground up there. Howard Bradbury was keeping time that spring. Yeah, we were in 32. That was a year. Road development was like that thing. Yeah, that's 32 in the spring, it was, yeah. That was just a start at the beginning. Well, that's a hell of a thing. Was that guy...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was something wrong with him or something that he did?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, I never could figure it out. Nobody ever did. Everybody tried to, but he missed the landing all right. And after that, he didn't... I tried a couple of other times, but he didn't make much... I knew myself he was going to miss some other ones. But the line broke, and she's what happened at the... Robert's in the first landing, and she broke the snow blind, you know. And lost a man off the raft, you see. The guy that went to shore, he could buy a raft here. He was there. But the broke the line, and... Oh, that is making a difference. He could have got in someplace. Well, it wasn't too high. It was up pretty well, but not... Hadn't had the big water yet, until the big water still came after that. But I never could figure out where he... But he got relieved with his command when the whole push caught up to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Without the snow blind, how would you stop it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, you'd have to improvise. With the more line... Oh, yes. It could pretty much rig up...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Anything. Well, there's places where you wouldn't need one. You could step off, you know. Or either you had to gallop, or you could get around something, you know, and get stopped. Oh, what... You were new, so you didn't know what was going on too good. We all knew. Oh, I see. You guys were waiting for your orders. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: There was no rasping on there, except, well, Bill Aikens. And this is the last one, he was supposed to be the big shot. But I don't know, he never made it as obsessive as I could see. That's the way it was. But after that day, well... Oh, yeah, Bill Aikens took him there. He ran him for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long, do you remember how long that first drive took?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: By gosh, no, you see it was split up, you know. It changed pushes in Big Island, you see. Another push went down from there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean the first drive?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, I don't remember, no. Casted away for the summer. No machinery or nothing, you know, everything. The water got away, and everything, the river was new to him. They got to leave water that year in June, but didn't hit, helped, of course, but it dropped right away again, yeah. We were down there in the big bars in the main river, cranking them logs over there by hand. Hot. That's not very interesting.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: What, you know, these log drives, I never did see one myself. And up north there, of course, we never had any, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the men working in the water on the logs, what did, what was the techniques that they used? What did they do to keep them logs moving what they have to do?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, it takes like the river crew, they ganged up around the log and rolled it in the water, rolled it in the river. That's all they could do. Just keep rolling until it went afloat. Then there was another crew, more experienced, and the, well, they weren't young men or they weren't old men. They were, been around 35, you know, they were in the center for a few days. They would take the floating stuff, you know, like you land on the center, you know, centered in the center of the river, you know. And it's held there by something, you know, rock or something, you know. When they pile up, they get to be really long, you know. But you make space in the front, get slack in front, you see. That's the first thing you got to do. Oh, it all depends on the water and general conditions, you know. You get slack and then it'll start to peel off, you see. Well, sometimes it'll go and sometimes it'll hang again and pile up more. That makes it bad, then. You've got to, when it starts to go, you'll hang out of it with the boat as long as you can, you see. Unless there's a shot or something in there and then you've got to get the hell away from you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A what?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: A shot, you know, powder. Sometimes you use powder, you see. But with these motorboats, you could always come back. But, if I told you couldn't, you could have to line them back. That took, well, a different length of time, of course. Sometimes that was an awful job, depends on how fast the water was and how far down river you went. You had to line it back. The crew would line it back up way above the center so you could land on it again, you know. That's why they didn't use so much powder, then, because you had to get away from it when you put a shot in, you know. They didn't want to leave it, you know. They wanted to stay and pick on it until it's still unrivaled, you see. If they were sure it was going to go, they'd leave it.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And, oftentimes they did. Most of the time, after you got psyched, they started picking on them a while, and they'd peel off, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say line them back up, will you explain that? What do you mean?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, they all got a, what they call a painter on them, you know, a rope in the bow. And, oh, it's probably into small rope, the gliding, maybe. Well, the boatmen stay in, and they pull, keep it offshore, and the rest of the crew will yell and get a hold of that line on the bank, and crawl along the bank and pull the goddamn thing back up. You see, that's, nobody likes to do that. That's why they hang out with them, send them so long, you see. They'd stay right on there, you know. They'd land in a place where the back pretty well from the front, you know, getting amongst, between the logs, you see, so they could stay there until it went. Then they'd go.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But I don't know. I'd go there three days and a half on spring. That's as long as it took. And I've driven her a hundred days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's, how do you get, how could you do it three and a half days? What were the conditions?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, the rear was at El, El Crick, you know what that is. Yeah, sure. Well, that's where the rear was, and I don't know. That was in,

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: forty -eight. And we stayed in town. We only had one boat. There was only four or five, six of us, maybe six, I think there was. But the water come up, the Lord God, you couldn't, you couldn't keep up to the rear. We had dinner at a socket that day from El Crick. You can figure that out. Then out into the main river we went. A while it was in the highway, you know. At Kerele, a while I was right in the highway. Webb was, of course, that year. Young Webb, Bill Webb, that's what dropped away some. He worked on the pond at the end. And the next, on a Sunday we finished up, we were at the, I don't know, I think we left a little lap away or something. There was nothing anywhere anymore. Three days in the habit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is he still alive?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yes. He was a push on that drive. A good push. He lives a...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Third, on third someplace. I know where he lives, but I can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how old is he? He retired now?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, he's retired. He was pulling around the pond out here for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, you gonna be around tomorrow?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, I can't tell you tomorrow. I'll be around any more. This lady that attended by down here, she lived two houses from profit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We're at the Silver Dollar?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. In the morning. She's there in the morning. Not tomorrow morning, but she's there on Tuesday, Wednesday. Well, I'm back here another time. I'll find out. Yeah. I haven't seen profit but once since he left the woods. He's on camp too, you know. Yeah. Yeah, he's a real guy. I didn't think he was that old. I drove with him on the Port of Lend River. Oh, Christ, I thought I was just as young as I did. He was too. He isn't as old as I am. He's about sixty -six, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was you born in?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Nineteen ... of April.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much, how much did it work when these guys were on the river working on the drive? Were they on the log standing right on it? Oh, is that a rare thing or was that pretty often you did see them there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It depends on what you were doing. If you were feeling a nanny or something like that, you'd be working on timber all the time. But the real crew there worked on logs, unless they wanted to be, you know, when they were meeting once in a while they'd get around and get in a slew or something. They'd get to the pool and round, but no, it wasn't too much work on loose timber except them centered. If you were picking a jam once in a while, you'd get on the stuff, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of the time they worked on the bateau, though?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, they'd be out of the bateau, of course. They wouldn't be in the bateau. They'd be out working on the timber. But the bateau would be there and pick them up in the case they went to drift or, you know, got hurt or got dinged up some way, you know, where there was any way in capacity to pick them up. Unless they got scattered. That's one thing a boatman didn't like, if they got scattered on, you know, too much, you see. Like a center breaking up, you know, and the crew gets scattered and they couldn't pick them all up. Maybe someone wasn't capable of handling themselves in that position, you know. They'd get excited and get out of hand like that. But mostly they got along pretty good. They lost about four men on this river. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see anybody go? Get lost?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah. I see. They had three years on over that spring. We wouldn't pick them up, but we were too late. We were just above them. They were on the right -hand side of the river going down just above the bridge. There was trees there, you know. And there was half a dozen bull pine trees scattered around there. And the water was maybe six feet deep, but also strong, you know. It was smooth, but running fast, you know. Strong water. You couldn't commence to swim against it or nothing like that, you know. You couldn't blow a boat against it, you know. Well, there was a boat working just below us on this one of these trees, you know, taking this timber off the trees. And when the center, when it moved out, you see, when they got slacked and everything, why, of course, it just picked up and left, and there was a big sweeper, big steerer. Oh, swiper, and the stump, full tree length, stump and everything, you know. Underneath that you couldn't see it. And it'd come up under the boat, you know, and swamp the boat. They'd come up on the bow and the stern went down, you know, and it just rolled around underneath. Well, there was these guys that ground her were in the boat. But there was four of them up the tree, went up this tree. Next.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, of course, they, I seen it. I was looking at it. Oh, I was near that house, I guess, up river in the other boat. We were working on the little center there. Someone said they were going to swamp that boat. They seen it coming back, you know. So we all jumped in. The boatman hollered first, jump in. There was only four of us. We had the four boats. And we took after them. You see, we left the guys in the tree. We didn't stop for them at all. We could see one guy. He had his head just going out of the bridge as we got closer. We couldn't get to him, and he disappeared. The water was high and awful, dirty, you know. You couldn't see nothing, you know. So I don't know. We landed, I guess, at Cottonwood Isle. There was some guy going down along the bank. He was in the water, too. I think that was the name of Chase. There was Fitty Chase and Anderson. They were the names of the guys that died. Miss Anderson, her, his daughter works in the liquor store at that old farm. She's 42 years old now. But anyhow, we landed at Cottonwood Isle on the highway side. It was on a Sunday, and oh, got a bit of traffic. But I got up there and stopped an old flatbed truck coming along. We put the boat out of these. Way longer than the old flatbed, but three or four of us got in the bow and held it down and tied it on. We had to come back up and land, put that boat in the water, and land on that creek. Where them guys were, that was a tricky proposition. But we made her tomorrow.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Or Ben Tom was one of them. He lives in Orofino now.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: He runs a, no, he don't run nothing now. He works for construction now. He's 63, I think, now. But he was one of the guys up the tree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what year was that?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It was 48, oh, 49.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Because me and this gal over here in the liquor store, you know, I get whiskey for the silver dollar, you know. I got a little venture over there, but then here it is. But anyhow, I used to talk to her, you know, she's a nice gal. I said, you knew my dad. That's when I first started getting whiskey there three or four years ago. I said, yes, sure. But I didn't know who she was talking about. I was shooting in the dark and she happened to wait till she volunteered some information, which she did right away, you know, she said to Walt Anderson. I knew Walter as well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So these three guys never had a chance of rolling?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, no, not after they got in the water. They didn't have no chance, no. No, no. Unless, the only chance they had was to stay with the boat. The boat didn't really swamp until it hit the pier, you know. It hit the bridge pier and then it went under. We found that down, around Cottonwood Creek, washed up on a bar, but it was not serviceable anymore, you know. But I don't know. There was spare paddles in that boat and everything underneath the seats. I tied them into myself when we started out. So I had two paddles tied underneath. There was nothing away or nothing. They could have grabbed one of them, you know. Even the boat, them boats would be just about ready to sink and they'd still pack three or four men, you know. But they could have done something if they had stayed with that boat. They could have stayed off of that pier, grabbed them paddles, and of course, always and everything was gone. Oh, I don't know.
